mappings.rationaleAI: Sea covers physical oceanography — seawater chemistry, currents, tides, waves — and the history of oceanography, squarely within B.3's hydrologic sciences (oceanography). It also connects to A.5.b.iv (ocean circulation, currents, and waves).

Geology is the core geologic science described in B.2, encompassing petrology, structural geology, stratigraphy, plate tectonics, and geochronology. Its disciplinary history maps across A.3.a–A.5.a for the 16th–20th century development of the field.

Pacific Ocean covers physical oceanography and marine geology relevant to B.3 (oceanography), with bathymetric and seafloor features connecting to A.5.b.iii (charting of ocean floors and progress in bathymetry).

Earth as a planet encompasses internal structure, hydrosphere, atmosphere, and geological history — together spanning the full scope of B's individual Earth sciences from physical geography (B.1) to geologic sciences (B.2).

Internal structure of Earth covers seismic wave analysis, core-mantle differentiation, and geomagnetic field generation, directly corresponding to A.5.a.iii (development of seismology and study of Earth's internal structure) and geophysics within B.2.

Ocean is the primary subject of B.3 (oceanography), covering physical oceanography, chemical properties, marine biology, and ocean circulation. Its study of currents, waves, and seawater chemistry maps to A.5.b.i and A.5.b.iv.

Geography maps directly to B.1 (physical geography — the study of distribution and spatial patterns of soils, water, climate, and landforms), covering GIS, remote sensing, and Earth science traditions traced from Greek scholarship.

Atlantic Ocean covers physical oceanography — Mid-Atlantic Ridge bathymetry, gyres, salinity patterns — relevant to B.3 and A.5.b.iii (charting ocean floors, bathymetry). Its geological formation also touches B.2 (plate tectonics).

Paleontology is explicitly listed in B.2's geologic sciences. A.4.a.ii (faunal succession and organic evolution) and A.5.a.v (advances in paleoecology, micropaleontology, Precambrian life) provide precise historical coverage.

Earth science defines the entire scope of this section, covering the nature and scope of all Earth science disciplines (B) and providing the broadest historical context for the field's development (A).

Mediterranean Sea includes oceanographic content (circulation, Messinian salinity crisis) relevant to B.3. Its tidal study connects to A.2.b.iii (study of the tides in antiquity through the 16th century), and its deep geological history to B.2.

Indian Ocean covers physical oceanography — monsoon-driven circulation, upwelling patterns, coral reef ecosystems — directly within B.3 (oceanography). Its geological youth and narrow continental shelves also connect to B.2.

History of Earth covers geologic time across four eons using geochronology (A.5.a.i — radiometric dating), stratigraphy, and the geological time scale, corresponding to B.2 and A.4.a.v (concept of geologic time and age of the Earth).

Land covers physical features of Earth's terrestrial surface including plate tectonics, terrain types, soil, and geomorphological processes, spanning B.1 (physical geography — soils, water, landforms) and B.2 (geomorphology).
Darwin is explicitly named in A.4.a.ii as a contributor to the concept of faunal succession and organic evolution, which was foundational to 19th-century geology and paleontology.

Arctic Ocean covers sea ice dynamics, polar oceanography, and high-latitude climate, fitting B.3 (oceanography, glaciology). Sea ice loss and methane clathrate risks connect to A.5.b.v (study of glacier motion and high-latitude ice sheets).

Lightning is an atmospheric electrical phenomenon studied under atmospheric sciences. It relates to A.3.c (early study of atmospheric phenomena) and A.4.c.ii (study of clouds, fog, dew, and storms in 19th-century atmospheric sciences).
Antarctica is central to B.3 (glaciology — Southern Ocean circulation, ice sheet dynamics) and A.5.b.v (study of glacier motion and high-latitude ice sheets). Its geology also connects to B.2.

Lakes are the primary subject of limnology, explicitly listed under B.3 (hydrologic sciences: hydrology, limnology). Their glacial origins also connect to B.2 (glacial geology) and A.4.a.iv (Ice Age evidence).

Rivers are a primary subject of hydrology (B.3), and A.3.b.i covers theories of spring discharge and early quantitative investigations of water balances. River geomorphology (erosion, deltas) also belongs to B.2.

Weather directly corresponds to A.5.c.i–ii (application of technology to meteorology, advances in weather forecasting) and A.4.c.iii (origin of synoptic meteorology). It is a core subject of the atmospheric sciences within B.

Rain (precipitation) connects to A.4.c.ii (study of clouds, fog, dew, and storms in 19th-century atmospheric sciences) and to B.3's hydrology as the primary freshwater delivery mechanism governing groundwater recharge and river systems.

Snow is a critical component of the cryosphere covered under B.3 (glaciology, hydrology), and its role in snowpack accumulation and ice formation directly connects to A.5.b.v (glacier motion and high-latitude ice sheets).

Rock is the foundational material of petrology and mineralogy, explicitly listed in B.2's geologic sciences. The classification of rocks connects to A.4.a.i (crystallography and classification of minerals and rocks) and A.5.a.ii (experimental petrology).

Climate covers classification systems (A.5.c.v), paleoclimatology, and climate variability central to B.1 (physical geography — climate) and A.4.c.iii (study of weather and climate, origin of synoptic meteorology).

History of science surveys Earth science milestones including plate tectonics, glacial theory, and paleoclimatology across all historical periods covered in A, providing broad historical context for the section's entire A path.
The Nile is explicitly named in A.2.b.ii (the origin of the Nile and the cause of its floods as a historical hydrological question) and connects to B.3 through hydrology and geomorphology.

Climate change relates to A.5.c (atmospheric sciences — atmospheric properties, weather modification, climate) and B.1 (physical geography — climate distribution and change). It brings together atmospheric and hydrological Earth sciences.

The Islamic Golden Age contributed substantially to medieval Earth sciences including hydrological theories and astronomical geography, fitting A.2's coverage of Earth sciences from antiquity to the 16th century including medieval contributions.

The Great Lakes constitute the world's largest freshwater lake system and are a prime subject of limnology, explicitly listed in B.3. Their glacial geological origins also connect to B.2 (glacial geology) and A.4.a.iv (Ice Age evidence).
Clouds are directly addressed in A.4.c.ii (study of clouds, fog, dew, and storms) and A.5.c.ii (advances in weather forecasting and cloud physics). They are central to atmospheric science within B.

Erosion is the primary geomorphological process studied in B.2 (geomorphology) and connects to A.4.a.vi (concepts of landform evolution in 19th-century geology) through its role in shaping Earth's surface.

Plate tectonics is explicitly named in A.5.a.vi as a unification of continental drift and seafloor spreading — the defining geologic advance of the 20th century. It is the foundational theory of B.2's structural geology.

Soil is an explicit subject of B.1 (physical geography — distribution and spatial patterns of soils) and connects to B.2 through pedology and weathering processes in environmental and engineering geology.

Earth's atmosphere is the core subject of atmospheric science within B, spanning all three historical phases: A.3.c (16th–18th century — water vapour, atmospheric pressure), A.4.c (19th century — composition, clouds, storms), and A.5.c (20th century — satellites, forecasting, climate).

The Age of Discovery drove early modern advances in physical geography, cartography, and hydrology through oceanographic exploration, fitting A.3's coverage of Earth sciences in the 16th and 17th centuries including beginnings of mineralogy and global water-balance investigations.

Continent covers geological definitions via continental crust and plate tectonics, Pangaea and continental drift, fitting B.1 (physical geography) and B.2 (structural geology, plate tectonics) as foundational physical geography and geological concepts.
